fact: February 26 is exactly 57 days into the year, which means there are only 308 days left until New Year's Eve!
In the Northern Hemisphere, this is when maple trees start getting ready to make syrup - their sap begins flowing faster as temperatures swing between freezing at night and warmer during the day.
February 26 falls smack in the middle of Pisces season, making it one of the most 'watery' days of the year according to astrology.
In Antarctica, this date marks the peak of summer - penguins are molting their old feathers and growing shiny new ones for winter.
Earthworms in temperate climates start wiggling closer to the surface around this time, preparing for their big spring emergence when the soil fully thaws.
The sun rises about 2 minutes earlier each day in late February, meaning February 26 has roughly 4 more minutes of daylight than February 24.
February 26 sits exactly 23 days before the spring equinox, making it the perfect 'countdown to spring' date in the Northern Hemisphere.
In many cold regions, this is prime 'sugar snow' season - the kind of perfect, sparkly snow that's ideal for making snow ice cream.
Hibernating bears are starting to stir in their dens around this time, even though they won't fully wake up for several more weeks.
February 26 marks the time when daylight in the Arctic begins returning rapidly - some northern towns gain over 6 minutes of daylight each day.
This date falls during the 'mud season' in many mountainous areas, when snow melts during the day but refreezes at night, creating weird ice sculptures everywhere.
